sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: Olfactory adaptation and detection threshold. Researchers examined whether repeated odor exposure changes detection threshold and perceived intensity using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 128 adults completing an odor-detection task before and after repeated low-intensity exposure and compared No exposure, Brief exposure, Repeated exposure. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in amount of repeated exposure to the target odor, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was odor concentration required for reliable detection; a secondary measure was perceived intensity at a fixed concentration. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring smoking status, allergy symptoms, and baseline threshold, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that repeated exposure increased the detection threshold and reduced perceived intensity at a fixed concentration. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to sensory adaptation and detection threshold, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, fatigue, expectation, or nasal congestion could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting sensory adaptation and detection threshold reported greater change in detection threshold and perceived odor intensity than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline allergy symptoms altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be counterbalancing odor identity and using forced-choice trials to reduce response-bias explanations. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.
questions.stem: Which interpretation of Figure 1 is most consistent with the investigators' central claim?

choices.A: The figure proves that fatigue, expectation, or nasal congestion played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The study cannot support any inference about sensory adaptation and detection threshold because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.
choices.C: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing sensory adaptation and detection threshold can alter odor concentration required for reliable detection in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.
choices.D: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures.

explanations.A: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on sensory adaptation and detection threshold, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'The figure proves that fatigue, expectation, or nasal congestion played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.' Yet the study manipulated amount of repeated exposure to the target odor and measured odor concentration required for reliable detection, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for fatigue, expectation, or nasal congestion, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.

A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. explanations.C: Correct. This answer best integrates the design, Figure 1, and the construct being tested. The passage says the groups differed in amount of repeated exposure to the target odor and that the primary outcome was odor concentration required for reliable detection; the figure then shows that repeated exposure increased the detection threshold and reduced perceived intensity at a fixed concentration. That combination supports the inference in the answer without overstating it as proof of every possible mechanism. It also respects the distinction between a construct, such as sensory adaptation and detection threshold, and the way that construct was operationalized through detection threshold and perceived odor intensity and the measured outcome. On MCAT-style P/S questions, the best answer usually matches the specific comparison and measurement strategy rather than choosing a familiar keyword in isolation.

The passage is therefore intended to support reasoning about construct validity, internal validity, and the difference between an association shown by a figure and a broader causal claim that would require additional evidence.

questions.stem: In this study, detection threshold and perceived odor intensity is best described as which type of variable?

choices.A: It is the independent variable only because all self-report measures are automatically assigned by the researcher rather than measured from participants.
choices.B: It is an operationalized measure of sensory adaptation and detection threshold, meaning it is a concrete indicator used to study a broader theoretical construct.
choices.C: It is a biological confound that makes the social construct irrelevant, since subjective ratings can never be used in behavioral research.
choices.D: It is the sampling frame, because any questionnaire completed before the outcome determines which participants entered the study. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the proposed mechanism rather than merely repeating the same outcome?

choices.A: Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to odor concentration required for reliable detection.
choices.B: Showing that baseline scores differed strongly between groups would strengthen the causal interpretation by making the groups easier to separate.
choices.C: Removing the manipulation check would strengthen the study because fewer measures always reduce the risk of measurement bias. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: Showing that changes in detection threshold and perceived odor intensity statistically mediate the effect of condition on odor concentration required for reliable detection would better connect the manipulation to the proposed mechanism.

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about sensory adaptation and detection threshold?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.C: The design does not fully rule out fatigue, expectation, or nasal congestion, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats sensory adaptation and detection threshold as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.C: Using only participants who responded most strongly would make the result more representative because it would maximize the size of the observed effect.
choices.D: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring odor concentration required for reliable detection over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.

questions.passage: Signal detection in low-prevalence image search. Researchers examined whether base-rate information changes sensitivity and response bias during a low-prevalence search task using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 170 trainees classifying simulated radiology images with low or moderate target prevalence and compared Low prevalence, Low + alert, Moderate prevalence. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in target prevalence and the presence of an alert cue, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was hit rate for detecting target findings; a secondary measure was false-alarm rate and decision confidence. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring visual-search experience and fatigue rating, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that low prevalence reduced hit rate, while an alert cue partially improved detection. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to signal detection, attention, and response bias, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, differences in image difficulty rather than prevalence-induced bias could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting signal detection, attention, and response bias reported greater change in hit rate, false-alarm rate, and confidence than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether visual-search experience altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be computing d-prime and criterion separately to determine whether alerting changes sensitivity or response threshold. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.
questions.stem: Which interpretation of Figure 1 is most consistent with the investigators' central claim?

choices.A: The figure proves that differences in image difficulty rather than prevalence-induced bias played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.

choices.C: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats signal detection, attention, and response bias as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing signal detection, attention, and response bias can alter hit rate for detecting target findings in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring hit rate for detecting target findings over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
choices.B: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.C: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation.
